query: assist control ventilation and respiratory alkalosis

retrieved_docs.title: Assist-Control Ventilation -- Clinical Significance -- Advantages of AC Mode
retrieved_docs.content: Increased patient comfort through the ability to trigger breaths as needed Operator-managed patient CO2 levels allow easy corrections for respiratory acidosis/alkalosis. Low work breathing for the patient

retrieved_docs.title: Ventilator Management(Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Assist Control Ventilation (AC)
retrieved_docs.content: The advantages of AC mode are increased comfort, easy corrections for respiratory acidosis/alkalosis, and low work breathing for the patient. Some disadvantages include that being a volume-cycled mode, pressures cannot be directly controlled, which may cause barotrauma, and the patient can develop hyperventilation with breath stacking, auto-PEEP, and respiratory alkalosis. For a full description of Assist Control, please review the article titled "Ventilation, Assist Control." [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Alkalosis -- Etiology -- Iatrogenic causes:
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperventilation in intubated patients on mechanical ventilation Extracorporeal membranous oxygenation

retrieved_docs.title: Assist-Control Ventilation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: AC mode is an excellent method to assure good ventilation. It is frequently used in cases of metabolic or respiratory acidosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Adjusting Ventilator Settings Based on ABG Results -- Issues of Concern -- Alkalosis
retrieved_docs.content: Alkalosis features a high arterial pH, which can arise from either a respiratory or metabolic source. Respiratory alkalosis arises from a low PaCO2. Reducing the respiratory rate allows more CO2 to accumulate in the alveoli, thereby increasing PaCO2 and improving respiratory alkalosis. Lowering the Vt can increase PaCO2 levels, but this measure must be taken cautiously to ensure adequate ventilation. Metabolic alkalosis is characterized by a low HCO3-. The remedy is correcting the underlying metabolic issue while ensuring ventilation is adjusted to avoid further alkalosis. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Adjusting Ventilator Settings Based on ABG Results -- Issues of Concern -- Acidosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis is characterized by a low arterial pH. Respiratory acidosis features a low arterial pH due to a high PaCO2. Increasing the respiratory rate helps remove CO2, thus lowering PaCO2. Care must be taken to avoid inducing respiratory alkalosis or auto-PEEP due to insufficient exhalation time. Increasing Vt can also enhance CO2 removal. However, excessive Vts can lead to volutrauma and barotrauma, underscoring the need for balance. Metabolic acidosis is characterized by a low HCO3-. Addressing the underlying metabolic cause while maintaining adequate ventilation is the principle of ventilator management in metabolic acidosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Assist-Control Ventilation -- Clinical Significance -- Disadvantages of AC Mode
retrieved_docs.content: The system is volume-cycled, and barotrauma is a concern in stiff lungs. Routinely following plateau pressures should help prevent this. If a patient is tachypneic or if not enough time is allowed for exhaling, the patient can develop breath stacking and auto-PEEP. In this process, not all volume is exhaled in every breath, which increases the amount of air in the patient’s lung and the plateau and intrathoracic pressure. This can lead to hypotension due to diminished venous return. Disconnecting the patient from the ventilator to allow enough time for complete exhalation and then readjusting the settings is a solution. Since the patient can initiate breaths, hyperventilation can lead to respiratory alkalosis. This may be solved by assuring good sedation.

retrieved_docs.title: Adjusting Ventilator Settings Based on ABG Results -- Definition/Introduction -- Acidosis vs alkalosis
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory acidosis is characterized by a low pH and a high PaCO2. The causes of this condition include hypoventilation from conditions such as respiratory depression, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and neuromuscular disorders. Metabolic acidosis is characterized by low pH and HCO3-. Causes may include conditions such as diabetic ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis, and renal failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Adjusting Ventilator Settings Based on ABG Results -- Definition/Introduction -- Acidosis vs alkalosis
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory alkalosis is characterized by a high pH and a low PaCO2. Causes include hyperventilation due to conditions such as anxiety, pain, and hypoxemia. Metabolic alkalosis is characterized by high pH and HCO3-. Possible etiologies include vomiting, diuretic use, and hypokalemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Alkalosis(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory alkalosis in itself is not life-threatening; however, the underlying etiology may be. Always look for and treat the source of the illness. Interventions to reduce pH directly are typically not necessary as there is no mortality benefit to this therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Adjusting Ventilator Settings Based on ABG Results -- Definition/Introduction -- pH
retrieved_docs.content: The pH measures the hydrogen ion concentration in the blood, indicating the acidity or alkalinity. The normal pH range is 7.35 to 7.45. A pH below 7.35 indicates acidosis, while a pH above 7.45 indicates alkalosis. This variable determines the acid-base balance and guides the diagnosis of respiratory and metabolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Ventilator Management(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since having a patient on mechanical ventilation allows a practitioner to modify the patient’s ventilation and oxygenation, it has an important role in acute hypoxic and hypercapnic respiratory failure as well as in severe metabolic acidosis or alkalosis. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Adjusting Ventilator Settings Based on ABG Results -- Definition/Introduction -- Compensation mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: The respiratory system adjusts ventilation to correct metabolic acid-base disturbances (respiratory compensation). An example is increasing ventilation to decrease PaCO2 in metabolic acidosis. Meanwhile, the kidneys adjust bicarbonate reabsorption and hydrogen ion excretion to correct respiratory acid-base disturbances (metabolic compensation). An example is increasing HCO3- reabsorption in chronic respiratory acidosis. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Alkalosis(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of metabolic alkalosis is targeted at treating the underlying pathology. If hyperventilation is intentional, monitor the arterial or venous blood gas values closely. In severe cases, pH may be directly reduced using acidic agents. However, this is not routinely done. Hyperventilation typically occurs in response to an insult such as hypoxia, metabolic acidosis, pain, anxiety, or increased metabolic demand.

retrieved_docs.title: Adjusting Ventilator Settings Based on ABG Results -- Definition/Introduction -- Base excess
retrieved_docs.content: Base excess (BE) measures the amount of excess or deficient base in the blood. The normal range is -2 to +2 mmol/L. A positive BE indicates metabolic alkalosis, while a negative BE indicates metabolic acidosis. BE helps assess the metabolic component of acid-base disorders. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Failure in Adults -- Treatment / Management -- Correction of Hypercapnia and Respiratory Acidosis
retrieved_docs.content: This may be achieved by treating the underlying cause or providing ventilatory support. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: Acid‑base imbalance (اختلال التوازن الحمضي القاعدي) refers to any disturbance in the normal pH regulation of the blood, encompassing both acidosis and alkalosis of metabolic and respiratory origin. Respiratory acidosis (تَقَلْوُن تنفسي) is a specific subtype of this broader category, occurring when hypoventilation leads to carbon dioxide retention, resulting in a decrease in blood pH. Understanding that respiratory acidosis is a form of acid‑base imbalance is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic interventions, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: placing respiratory alkalosis as a higher‑order classification within the spectrum of acid‑base disorders. The condition الحماض (acidosis) is also defined as a type of حالات اضطراب توازن السائل والإلكتروليت والقاعدي الحمضي and is known to co‑occur with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم, underscoring the frequent simultaneous presence of acidosis and hyperkalemia in patients with severe fluid‑electrolyte disturbances. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the complex network of metabolic, respiratory, and toxicological factors that influence fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base homeostasis in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1701
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is a specific adult respiratory condition that is classified under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is classified as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, indicating that this procedure involves the insertion of an oral and pharyngeal airway to establish and maintain a secure airway for assisted ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperosmolality and hypernatriemia are classified as part of the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance, reflecting their role as disturbances in plasma osmolality and sodium concentration that impact overall homeostatic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is the overarching respiratory disturbance that encompasses the broader category of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ). Within this category, the clinical condition described as respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) represents a more severe manifestation. Thus, adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest are subtypes of the respiratory disorder, which itself is nested under the primary concept of apnea, illustrating a hierarchical relationship that links a fundamental breathing abnormality to increasingly critical respiratory pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1554
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SDTM Procedure Terminology includes the concept of respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation, which are essential airway management interventions used to secure the airway and provide assisted breathing in critically ill patients. It also incorporates the concept of thoracoscopic wedge resection of the lung, a minimally invasive surgical procedure performed to remove a small, wedge‑shaped portion of lung tissue for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. By encompassing both of these procedures, the CDISC SDTM Procedure Terminology provides a standardized framework for capturing and reporting these distinct clinical interventions in clinical trial data, facilitating consistent documentation and analysis across studies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1176
retrieved_docs.content: Collectively, these interconnected concepts illustrate the hierarchical, methodological and risk‑related links among bronchoscopy, nuclear medicine, airway management, respiratory therapy, various catheterization and intubation techniques, and their relevance to comprehensive patient assessment, monitoring and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 869
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation encompasses the specific procedure of تنبيب الرغامى, indicating that this intubation technique is a subcategory within the broader practice of managing the airway for ventilation. In addition, تنبيب الرغامى is also linked to trachea and bronchus operations, reflecting its relevance to surgical interventions involving the trachea and bronchi. Together, these relationships highlight that تنبيب الرغامى serves both as a component of respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation and as a procedure associated with tracheal and bronchial surgery, underscoring its importance in comprehensive airway management and operative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2566
retrieved_docs.content: Nonmechanical method of resuscitation encompasses manual techniques for reviving patients, and its specific child concept is إنعاش قلبي رئوي (cardiopulmonary resuscitation), which represents the hands‑on approach used to restore breathing and circulation without reliance on mechanical devices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Medical procedure on respiratory tract encompasses respiratory therapy (المعالجة التنفسية) as a specific category of intervention. Within this category, the technique of delivering oxygen via an extracorporeal membrane (إيصال الأكسجين عبر غشاء خارج الجسم) is identified as a child procedure of respiratory therapy, indicating that extracorporeal membrane oxygenation is a specialized form of respiratory therapy that falls under the broader umbrella of medical procedures targeting the respiratory tract.
